refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and responsive remote equipment control.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness and control responsiveness. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is a hard constraint for safe remote operation. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (not just network transit), encompasses both uplink and downlink paths, and is conditioned on agreed network assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit verification and traceability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Conduct operator acceptance testing with simulated remote tasks. Validate p95 latency across multiple network scenarios and load conditions. Establish continuous monitoring in field deployment.
sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, video, environmental) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator commands at vehicle with bounded network latency
FR-TEL-004: Execute operator commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing latency
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health to safety services

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency edge processing on vehicle with local sensor buffering, compressed telemetry streaming, and predictive command execution to minimize round-trip latency
DP-B: Deploy dedicated low-latency network infrastructure (private 5G or dedicated fiber) with QoS prioritization for teleoperation traffic and redundant communication paths
DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing (DP-A) with network optimization (DP-B) and operator-side latency compensation (command prediction, dead-reckoning display)
DP-D: Implement software-defined networking with dynamic latency monitoring and automatic failover to alternate communication paths when latency exceeds threshold
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid approach combining edge processing on vehicle (sensor fusion, data compression, local command execution), network optimization (QoS prioritization, redundant paths), and operator-side latency compensation (predictive display, command buffering). This approach addresses latency across all system layers: sensor acquisition, network transmission, and actuator response.

potential_sources: Coupling between sensor acquisition rate and network bandwidth (mitigated by compression)
Coupling between operator command frequency and vehicle processing capacity (mitigated by local buffering)
Coupling between network conditions and latency compensation algorithms (managed via monitoring and adaptation)
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits loose coupling across functional layers. Edge processing decouples sensor acquisition from network transmission. Latency compensation decouples operator perception from actual network delay. Redundant paths decouple from single network provider. Hybrid approach is preferred over single-layer solutions (DP-A or DP-B alone) because latency is a system-level property requiring multi-layer optimization.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%)
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle has sufficient compute capacity for edge processing and local command execution
ASSUMPTION: Operator interface supports latency compensation features (predictive display, command buffering)
RISK: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters in field deployment, violating latency SLA
RISK: Edge processing adds complexity and potential failure modes; local execution errors may not be immediately visible to remote operator
RISK: Latency compensation (prediction, dead-reckoning) may diverge from actual vehicle state, creating safety hazards
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly include: (1) latency monitoring and alerting thresholds, (2) fallback modes when latency exceeds safe limits (e.g., autonomous hold, return-to-safe-state), (3) operator training on latency compensation behavior, (4) network SLA enforcement mechanisms.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns end-to-end teleoperation latency performance. Encompasses sensor data acquisition and compression on vehicle, network transmission optimization, operator command reception and execution, and latency monitoring/compensation logic. Interfaces with Vehicle (for sensor/actuator access), Safety Services (for latency health reporting), and Network Link (for communication).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Compressed sensor data (position, video, environmental) streamed from vehicle to operator. Latency-critical; must be optimized for low-latency transmission.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands received from remote operator. Must be processed with minimal latency and executed on vehicle actuators.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Latency health status and alerts sent to Safety Services. Triggers when latency exceeds thresholds or network conditions degrade.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network communication (5G, fiber, or hybrid). Teleoperation module optimizes QoS, prioritization, and redundancy over this link.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own latency performance. Latency is a cross-cutting concern that would be diffused across multiple sub-modules if assigned here. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle owns sensor acquisition and actuator execution, which are components of latency. However, Vehicle does not own network transmission or operator-side latency compensation. Assigning latency to Vehicle would diffuse responsibility across Vehicle and operator infrastructure. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency. It encompasses sensor-to-operator-to-actuator path, network optimization, and latency compensation. Minimal diffusion; all latency-critical logic is contained within this module. Highest containment score.

Safety Services monitors and enforces safety constraints (including latency thresholds) but does not own the latency performance itself. Assigning latency to Safety Services would create circular dependency and diffuse responsibility. Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert from Teleoperation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008 (Teleoperation latency). It has the highest containment score (0.95) and lowest diffusion score (0.05). All latency-critical functions (sensor compression, network optimization, command execution, latency compensation) are naturally contained within Teleoperation's scope. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle for sensor/actuator latency. If Vehicle introduces unexpected delays, Teleoperation cannot fully control end-to-end latency. Mitigation: Define sensor acquisition and actuator response time SLAs in Vehicle interface contract.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for transmission latency. If network conditions degrade beyond agreed parameters, Teleoperation cannot meet p95 ≤ 150 ms. Mitigation: Establish network SLA enforcement and fallback modes (e.g., autonomous hold) when latency exceeds safe limits.

RISK: Latency compensation logic (prediction, dead-reckoning) may create safety hazards if operator perception diverges from actual vehicle state. Mitigation: Implement operator alerts and training on latency compensation behavior; include safety validation in verification plan.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation module boundary to explicitly include: (1) latency monitoring and alerting subsystem, (2) fallback/safe-state logic when latency exceeds thresholds, (3) operator interface for latency compensation feedback. Define clear interface contracts with Vehicle (sensor/actuator latency SLAs) and Network Link (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss parameters).

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP-C (hybrid approach) directly addresses all aspects of refined FR: edge processing reduces sensor-to-transmission latency, network optimization reduces transmission latency, and latency compensation reduces operator-to-actuator latency. DP-C is neither over-constrained nor under-constrained. Coupling analysis shows acceptable loose coupling across layers. Assumptions and risks are documented and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle for sensor/actuator latency; ensure Vehicle interface contract includes latency SLAs
Teleoperation module depends on Network Link for transmission latency; ensure network SLA enforcement and fallback modes are implemented
Latency compensation logic may create safety hazards; ensure Safety Services monitors and enforces latency thresholds
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) with minimal diffusion (0.05). All latency-critical logic is owned by Teleoperation. External dependencies (Vehicle, Network Link, Safety Services) are well-defined via interfaces. Ownership is clear and traceable.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under agreed network conditions, operator acceptance testing, and continuous monitoring in field deployment. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation (with mocked Vehicle and Network Link) and in integration with other modules.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns CR-NGHE-008 (Teleoperation latency). Sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005) are allocated to Teleoperation and its sub-components (sensor acquisition, network transmission, command execution, latency monitoring). Traceability from FR to DP to IM is established.

recommended_revisions: Define explicit latency SLAs in Vehicle interface contract (sensor acquisition latency, actuator response latency)
Define explicit network SLA parameters in Network Link interface contract (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, availability)
Implement latency monitoring and alerting subsystem within Teleoperation module; integrate with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface
Develop fallback/safe-state logic for when latency exceeds safe thresholds (e.g., autonomous hold, return-to-safe-state)
Include operator training and interface design for latency compensation behavior (predictive display, command buffering)

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further functional decomposition. The refined FR identifies five sub-FRs (FR-TEL-001 through FR-TEL-005) that must be decomposed to design parameters and implementation modules. Each sub-FR addresses a distinct latency component (sensor acquisition, network transmission, command reception, actuator execution, latency monitoring) and requires separate design and verification. Decomposition is necessary to achieve detailed design and implementation clarity.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TEL-001: Acquire sensor data (position, video, environmental) with minimal capture-to-transmission latency (target: ≤ 30 ms)
FR-TEL-002: Transmit sensor telemetry to remote operator with bounded network latency (target: ≤ 60 ms for p95 under agreed network conditions)
FR-TEL-003: Receive operator commands at vehicle with bounded network latency (target: ≤ 60 ms for p95 under agreed network conditions)
FR-TEL-004: Execute operator commands on vehicle actuators with minimal processing latency (target: ≤ 30 ms)
FR-TEL-005: Monitor and report teleoperation latency health to safety services (real-time monitoring, alert on threshold exceedance)

assumptions: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (bandwidth ≥ X Mbps, jitter ≤ Y ms, packet loss ≤ Z%)
Vehicle has sufficient compute capacity for edge processing and local command execution
Operator interface supports latency compensation features (predictive display, command buffering)
Safety Services can enforce latency thresholds and trigger fallback modes
Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation and in integration with Vehicle, Network Link, and Safety Services
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters in field deployment, violating latency SLA
Edge processing adds complexity and potential failure modes; local execution errors may not be immediately visible to remote operator
Latency compensation (prediction, dead-reckoning) may diverge from actual vehicle state, creating safety hazards
Operator training and interface design for latency compensation behavior is critical; inadequate training may lead to unsafe operation
Fallback/safe-state logic must be robust and tested; failure to trigger fallback when latency exceeds safe limits could result in loss of control
evidence_needed: Network SLA definition and enforcement mechanisms (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, availability)
Vehicle interface contract with latency SLAs (sensor acquisition latency, actuator response latency)
Latency monitoring and alerting subsystem design and implementation
Fallback/safe-state logic design and verification (autonomous hold, return-to-safe-state)
Operator training materials and interface design for latency compensation behavior
End-to-end latency measurement data under agreed network conditions (p95 ≤ 150 ms validation)
Operator acceptance testing results with simulated remote tasks
Field deployment monitoring data and latency performance metrics
